CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a clear logical flow (Intro → Related Work → Method (activation- and gradient-based) → Experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions are explicitly listed early in the Introduction. The notation is largely consistent and introduced where needed (e.g. A ∈ R^{C×H×W}, F : R^{C×H×W}→R^{H×W}, Q vectors and normalization in Eq. 2), so methods are understandable. Presentation suffers from minor issues: figures/tables are frequently referenced (Fig. 3–7, Table 1–3) but not all visuals appear in the provided text and the Appendix contains duplicated/garbled paragraphs and LaTeX artifacts. Overall the writing is clear with only minor grammatical/formatting glitches that should be fixed for polish (repetitions in the Appendix, some inline formatting errors).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (attention defined as spatial maps, transfer via activation- and gradient-based maps improves student networks) and provides empirical evidence across CIFAR, CUB/Scenes and ImageNet (e.g. Table 2 medians, reported +1.1% top-1 on ImageNet). The mathematical formulation and derivatives (Eqns. 2–5) are correct and appropriately connect to double backpropagation. Experimental methodology is generally sound with relevant baselines (KD, FitNets/full-activation transfer) and multiple architectures, but some weaknesses remain: limited runs/statistical testing (single-run settings for some CIFAR gradient experiments), admitted hyperparameter/tuning shortcuts on ImageNet and unresolved practical issues (batch-norm behavior in second backprop). The main assumptions (e.g. using absolute activation magnitudes as attention) are empirically motivated but not formally justified; nonetheless the conclusions are supported by the presented results.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a new mechanism for knowledge transfer — forcing a student network to mimic a teacher’s spatial "attention" maps — and instantiates this with activation-based mappings (Fsum, F^psum, F^pmax) and gradient-based attention (eqs. 1–2 and 3–5). This is clearly differentiated from prior distillation and hint methods (FitNets, KD) by operating on normalized spatial attention maps rather than full activations, and by using double backpropagation and a flip-symmetry term for gradient-based AT (eq. 6). While building on existing visualization and regularization ideas (e.g., Simonyan et al., Drucker & LeCun), it is a non-trivial and practically meaningful extension that would be informative to experts (including demonstrated ImageNet transfers).
